name: PLS Check Cashers
description_short: At 2335 E 38th St, Indianapolis, IN 46218, PLS Check Cashers can be reached at +1 317-931-8000.
description_long:
PLS Financial Services operates a network of Community Currency Exchange locations across 12 states — Arizona, California, Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Massachusetts, New York, North Carolina, Ohio, Oklahoma, Texas, and Wisconsin. The company has been serving unbanked and underbanked consumers through high-street storefronts that combine financial and government services under one roof, open around the clock.

At its core, PLS offers a full suite of transactional financial services: check cashing, money orders, bill payment, Ria Money Transfer for international remittances, government benefits processing, and foreign currency exchange. Beyond money services, Illinois locations operate as Community Currency Exchanges authorized to handle vehicle registration and renewals, title transfers, license plate issuance, and city stickers — services typically requiring a trip to a government office. The Xpectations PLUS Debit Mastercard program provides a prepaid card with a companion mobile app as a banking alternative for consumers without a traditional bank account.

What sets PLS apart from a basic check casher is the breadth of non-financial services layered on top. The combination of DMV-equivalent transactions, notary services, fax and copy, phone top-ups, and ATM access means a single visit can accomplish tasks that would otherwise require multiple stops. All service categories are listed as open 24/7, making PLS accessible to shift workers, overnight travelers, and anyone who cannot reach a bank during business hours.

For consumers who are unbanked or underbanked, PLS is a pragmatic utility hub rather than a wealth-building tool. Check cashing fees are not disclosed on the website — industry rates typically range from 1% to 5% of the check face value, which can add up over time compared to a free checking account. PLS does not offer loans or credit products, so it serves transactional needs only. Consumers with access to a traditional bank or credit union will generally find lower-cost alternatives for most of these services, but for those without that access or those who need services outside banking hours, PLS fills a genuine gap.
pros: All service categories — money, transportation, debit card, business, other — listed as open 24/7
Ria Money Transfer available in-store for international remittances without a separate visit
Illinois locations handle vehicle registration, title transfers, and license plates — saving a DMV trip
Xpectations PLUS Debit Mastercard with mobile app gives unbanked consumers a card-based alternative
Foreign exchange available for currency conversion needs
Government benefits processing available at the same location
Notary services available for wills, deeds, power of attorney, and legal documents
cons: Check cashing fees are not disclosed on the website — consumers must visit a store to learn the cost
No lending or credit products offered — not useful if you need cash beyond what a check provides
Primarily serves high-cost transactional needs; consumers with a bank account will find cheaper alternatives for most services
Foreign exchange rates and money transfer fees are not published online
Xpectations debit card terms, fees, and conditions are not detailed on the public site
best_for: Unbanked or underbanked consumers who need immediate cash from a paycheck or government check
Illinois residents needing vehicle registration renewals or title transfers without a weekday DMV visit
Consumers sending money internationally via Ria Money Transfer
Night-shift workers or anyone needing financial services outside standard banking hours
diagnosis: PLS Check Cashers is best for unbanked consumers or anyone who needs immediate check access, bill payment, or international money transfers at any hour of the day. Illinois residents also benefit from the in-store DMV-equivalent services. The main caveat is that fees for check cashing and transfers are not disclosed publicly and are typically higher than what a bank or credit union would charge, making this a convenience play rather than a cost-efficient one.
